periods.objective: Calculate area of triangles, parallelograms, and trapezoids.
periods.workedExamples: 6-1, Examples 1-3 — Area of Triangle; Area of Parallelogram; Area of Trapezoid
periods.notes: Core area formulas for standard polygons.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6a with one retrieval prompt recalling the area formulas for a rectangle and a triangle.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-1, Examples 1-3 to develop: apply A = ½bh for triangles, A = bh for parallelograms, and A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h for trapezoids, identifying base and height in each figure.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-1, Examples 1-3: identify bases and heights and compute areas for each polygon type; teacher checks height identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on area_standard_polygons: area calculations for triangles, parallelograms, and trapezoids.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one area calculation for each of the three polygon types.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with area calculations for standard polygons.

periods.objective: Calculate area of regular polygons using the apothem and find area of composite figures.
periods.workedExamples: 6-1, Examples 4-6 — Area of Regular Hexagon Using Apothem; Composite Figure Area; Real-World: Carpeting a Room with Irregular Shape
periods.notes: Apothem-based area, composite figures, and real-world application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6b with one retrieval prompt defining the apothem of a regular polygon.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-1, Examples 4-6 to develop: apply A = ½ × perimeter × apothem for a regular hexagon, decompose composite figures into standard shapes and sum areas, and apply composite area to a real-world room-carpeting problem.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-1, Examples 4-6: compute apothem-based areas and decompose composite figures; teacher checks decomposition strategy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on area_composite_figures: apothem-based and composite figure area problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one apothem-based area problem and one composite figure area problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with apothem and composite figure areas.

periods.objective: Compute surface area of prisms, cylinders, and pyramids.
periods.workedExamples: 6-2, Examples 1-3 — Surface Area of Rectangular Prism; Surface Area of Cylinder; Surface Area of Square Pyramid
periods.notes: Surface area of rectangular prism, cylinder, and square pyramid.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6c with one retrieval prompt on what surface area means and how it differs from volume.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-2, Examples 1-3 to develop: compute the surface area of a rectangular prism by summing six faces, compute cylinder surface area (two circles + lateral rectangle), and compute square pyramid surface area (four triangles + base).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-2, Examples 1-3: draw nets and compute surface areas for each solid; teacher checks net accuracy and formula use.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on surface_area_prisms_pyramids: surface area calculations for prisms, cylinders, and pyramids.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one surface area calculation for a rectangular prism and one for a cylinder.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with surface area calculations.

periods.objective: Compute surface area of cones and spheres and solve real-world surface area problems.
periods.workedExamples: 6-2, Examples 4-6 — Surface Area of Cone; Surface Area of Sphere; Real-World: Amount of Paint for a Silo
periods.notes: Surface area of cone, sphere, and composite real-world application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6d with one retrieval prompt on the surface area formulas for a cone and a sphere.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-2, Examples 4-6 to develop: compute cone surface area (πr² + πrl), compute sphere surface area (4πr²), and apply surface area to find the amount of paint needed for a composite silo structure.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-2, Examples 4-6: compute surface areas and solve real-world problems; teacher checks slant height identification for cones.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on surface_area_cones_spheres: cone, sphere, and composite real-world surface area problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one cone surface area calculation and one sphere surface area calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with cone and sphere surface area.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

periods.objective: Calculate volume of prisms and cylinders.
periods.workedExamples: 6-3, Examples 1-3 — Volume of Rectangular Prism; Volume of Triangular Prism; Volume of Cylinder
periods.notes: Core volume formulas: rectangular prism, triangular prism, and cylinder using V = Bh.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6e with one retrieval prompt on the volume formula V = Bh (base area × height).
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-3, Examples 1-3 to develop: compute volume of a rectangular prism using V = lwh, compute volume of a triangular prism using V = ½bh × H, and compute cylinder volume using V = πr²h.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-3, Examples 1-3: compute base area and multiply by height for each solid type; teacher checks base area calculation.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on volume_prisms_cylinders: volume calculations for rectangular prisms, triangular prisms, and cylinders.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one rectangular prism volume and one cylinder volume calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with prism and cylinder volume.

periods.objective: Compare volumes and apply prism/cylinder volume to real-world problems.
periods.workedExamples: 6-3, Examples 4-5 — Volume Comparison: Which Container Holds More?; Real-World: Water in a Cylindrical Tank
periods.notes: Volume comparison between container types and real-world cylindrical tank application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6f with one retrieval prompt comparing the base areas of a rectangle and a circle to set up a volume comparison.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-3, Examples 4-5 to develop: compare volumes of a rectangular prism container and a cylinder container by computing both and finding the difference, and apply cylinder volume to a real-world water-tank problem with unit conversion (ft³ to gallons).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-3, Examples 4-5: compute volumes for comparison and apply unit conversions; teacher checks comparison setup and conversion factors.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on volume_prisms_cylinders: volume comparisons and real-world cylinder problems with unit conversion.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one volume comparison problem and one real-world cylinder volume problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with volume comparisons and unit conversions.

periods.notes: Mathematical practices focus: choosing between area, surface area, and volume formulas; unit analysis; error analysis in measurement.

nonInstructionArtifact.groupTask: Mathematical practices focus: choosing between area, surface area, and volume formulas; unit analysis; error analysis in measurement.
nonInstructionArtifact.sourceWorkedExamples: Use the prior instruction periods named in the notes as the problem bank.
nonInstructionArtifact.deliverable: Group solution poster or shared explanation with one strategy comparison.
nonInstructionArtifact.facilitationNotes: Assign roles for formula selection, computation, unit checking, and explanation.
nonInstructionArtifact.completionCriteria: Correct method, accurate result, clear justification, and response to peer question.

periods.notes: Mid-unit review across 6a-6f. Covers area of polygons, surface area of solids, and prism/cylinder volume.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine area, surface area, and volume in multi-step measurement problems.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.

periods.workedExamples: 6-4, Examples 1-2 — Volume of Cone; Volume of Sphere
periods.notes: Cone volume and sphere volume formulas.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6g with one retrieval prompt on the volume formulas for a cone and a sphere.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-4, Examples 1-2 to develop: compute cone volume as ⅓πr²h, and compute sphere volume as ⁴⁄₃πr³.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-4, Examples 1-2: compute cone and sphere volumes; teacher checks formula selection and fraction arithmetic.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on volume_cones_spheres: cone volume and sphere volume problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one cone volume calculation and one sphere volume calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with cone and sphere volume.

periods.objective: Calculate volume of spheres and compare cone-cylinder volumes.
periods.workedExamples: 6-4, Examples 3-4 — Compare Cone and Cylinder with Same Base and Height; Volume of Hemisphere
periods.notes: Cone-cylinder volume comparison and hemisphere volume.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6h with one retrieval prompt on the relationship between cone and cylinder volumes with the same base and height.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-4, Examples 3-4 to develop: compare a cone and cylinder with the same base and height to verify the ⅓ relationship, and compute hemisphere volume as half of sphere volume (⅔πr³).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-4, Examples 3-4: compute cone-cylinder ratios and hemisphere volumes; teacher checks the ⅓ relationship and hemisphere formula.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on volume_cones_spheres: cone-cylinder comparison and hemisphere volume problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one cone-cylinder comparison problem and one hemisphere volume calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with volume comparisons and hemisphere calculations.

periods.objective: Apply volume formulas to composite solids and real-world problems.
periods.workedExamples: 6-4, Examples 5-6 — Real-World: Ice Cream Cone Capacity; Composite Solid Volume
periods.notes: Real-world capacity problems and composite solid volume.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6i with one retrieval prompt on how to break a composite solid into its component shapes.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-4, Examples 5-6 to develop: find the total capacity of an ice cream cone by summing the cone and hemisphere volumes, and decompose composite solids into recognizable components (prisms, cylinders, cones, spheres, hemispheres) to compute total volume.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-4, Examples 5-6: solve real-world capacity problems and decompose composite solids; teacher checks decomposition and volume formulas.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on volume_composite_solids: real-world and composite solid volume problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one real-world capacity problem and one composite solid volume problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with composite solid and real-world volume.

periods.workedExamples: 6-5, Examples 1-2 — Cross-Sections of a Cube; Cross-Sections of a Cylinder
periods.notes: Core cross-section identification: cube and cylinder cross-sections for various slicing orientations.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6j with one retrieval prompt on what a cross-section is when slicing a 3D figure.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-5, Examples 1-2 to develop: identify cross-sections of a cube when sliced parallel to a face (square), perpendicular through the center (rectangle), and through six edges (regular hexagon); identify cross-sections of a cylinder when sliced parallel to the base (circle), perpendicular to the base (rectangle), and at a 45° angle (ellipse).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-5, Examples 1-2: sketch cross-sections from slicing planes for cubes and cylinders; teacher checks cross-section identification and shape accuracy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on cross_sections: cross-section identification for various slicing orientations.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one cube cross-section identification and one cylinder cross-section identification.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with identifying cross-sections.

periods.objective: Apply cross-sections to composite solid volume.
periods.workedExamples: 6-5, Example 3 — Volume of Composite Solid (Cylinder + Hemisphere)
periods.notes: Computing volume of capsule-shaped composite solid using cross-section decomposition.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6k with one retrieval prompt on how to decompose a capsule shape into a cylinder and two hemispheres.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-5, Example 3 to develop: compute the volume of a capsule-shaped composite solid (cylinder + two hemispheres) by identifying cross-sections at each height and summing component volumes.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-5, Example 3: decompose composite solids and compute total volume; teacher checks decomposition.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on cross_sections: composite solid volume problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one composite solid volume problem requiring at least two components.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with composite solid volume.

periods.objective: Apply Cavalieri's principle and connect cross-sections to real-world problems.
periods.workedExamples: 6-5, Examples 4-5 — Cavalieri's Principle Application; Real-World: 3D Printing Cross-Sections
periods.notes: Cavalieri's principle for comparing volumes and 3D printing layer-by-layer analysis.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 6l with one retrieval prompt on Cavalieri's principle: if two solids have equal cross-sectional areas at every height, they have equal volume.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 6-5, Examples 4-5 to develop: apply Cavalieri's principle to show an oblique cylinder has the same volume as a right cylinder with the same base and height, and analyze how a 3D printer builds a sphere layer by layer with varying circular cross-sections.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 6-5, Examples 4-5: apply Cavalieri's principle and connect cross-sections to 3D printing layer analysis; teacher checks principle application.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on cross_sections: Cavalieri's principle and real-world cross-section problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one Cavalieri's principle application and one 3D printing cross-section analysis.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with Cavalieri's principle and real-world cross-sections.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.
